title: Reported speech
lesson: When we report what someone said, the tense usually shifts back one step (present simple to past simple, present perfect and past simple to past perfect, will to would, can to could). Pronouns change to match the new speaker's point of view, and time/place words shift too (now to then, today to that day, tomorrow to the next day, here to there). Reported questions use statement word order with no question mark, using 'if/whether' for yes/no questions. Reported commands use 'tell/ask someone to do' something.

answer: She said (that) she was tired.
She said that she was tired.
She said she was tired.
items.explanation: Present simple 'am' backshifts to past simple 'was', and 'I' becomes 'she'.

items.prompt: "We have finished the project," they told me. (reported speech)
answer: They told me (that) they had finished the project.
They told me that they had finished the project.
They told me they had finished the project.
items.explanation: Present perfect 'have finished' backshifts to past perfect 'had finished', and 'we' becomes 'they'.

items.prompt: "I will call you tomorrow," he said. (reported speech)
answer: He said (that) he would call me the next day.
He said that he would call me the next day.
He said he would call me the next day.
He said (that) he would call me the following day.
He said he would call me the following day.
items.explanation: 'Will' backshifts to 'would', 'you' becomes 'me', and 'tomorrow' becomes 'the next day' / 'the following day'.

items.prompt: "I saw her yesterday," Tom said. (reported speech)
answer: Tom said (that) he had seen her the day before.
Tom said that he had seen her the day before.
Tom said he had seen her the day before.
Tom said (that) he had seen her the previous day.
Tom said he had seen her the previous day.
items.explanation: Past simple 'saw' backshifts to past perfect 'had seen', and 'yesterday' becomes 'the day before' / 'the previous day'.

items.prompt: "I can swim very well," the boy said. (reported speech)
answer: The boy said (that) he could swim very well.
The boy said that he could swim very well.
The boy said he could swim very well.
items.explanation: The modal 'can' backshifts to 'could', and 'I' becomes 'he'.

items.prompt: "I am working hard today," she said. (reported speech)
answer: She said (that) she was working hard that day.
She said that she was working hard that day.
She said she was working hard that day.
items.explanation: Present continuous 'am working' backshifts to past continuous 'was working', and 'today' becomes 'that day'.

items.prompt: "Where do you live?" she asked me. (reported question)
answer: She asked me where I lived.
She asked where I lived.
items.explanation: Reported questions use statement word order (no auxiliary 'do' and no question mark); 'live' backshifts to 'lived'.

items.prompt: "Have you seen my keys?" he asked. (reported question)
answer: He asked if I had seen his keys.
He asked whether I had seen his keys.
items.explanation: Yes/no questions are reported with 'if' or 'whether'; present perfect 'have seen' backshifts to 'had seen' and 'my' becomes 'his'.

items.prompt: "Are you coming to the party?" she asked him. (reported question)
answer: She asked him if he was coming to the party.
She asked him whether he was coming to the party.
items.explanation: The yes/no question uses 'if/whether' with statement word order; 'are you coming' becomes 'he was coming'.

items.prompt: "Close the window, please," she said to him. (reported command)
answer: She asked him to close the window.
She told him to close the window.
items.explanation: Reported requests and commands use 'ask/tell someone to do' something; the imperative becomes a to-infinitive.

items.prompt: "Don't touch the stove," the mother said to the child. (reported command)
answer: The mother told the child not to touch the stove.
The mother asked the child not to touch the stove.
items.explanation: Negative commands are reported with 'not to do' something after 'tell/ask someone'.

items.prompt: "I went to the doctor last week," he said. (reported speech)
answer: He said (that) he had gone to the doctor the week before.
He said that he had gone to the doctor the week before.
He said he had gone to the doctor the week before.
He said (that) he had gone to the doctor the previous week.
He said he had gone to the doctor the previous week.
items.explanation: Past simple 'went' backshifts to past perfect 'had gone', and 'last week' becomes 'the week before' / 'the previous week'.

items.prompt: "Why are you sitting here?" the teacher asked the boy. (reported question)
answer: The teacher asked the boy why he was sitting there.
The teacher asked why he was sitting there.
items.explanation: The wh- question keeps statement word order; 'are you sitting' backshifts to 'he was sitting' and the place word 'here' becomes 'there'.

items.prompt: "I must finish this report today," she said. (reported speech)
answer: She said (that) she had to finish that report that day.
She said that she had to finish that report that day.
She said she had to finish that report that day.
items.explanation: The modal 'must' (obligation) backshifts to 'had to', 'this' becomes 'that', and 'today' becomes 'that day'.

items.prompt: "What time does the next train leave?" the tourist asked. (reported question)
answer: The tourist asked what time the next train left.
The tourist asked what time the next train departed.
items.explanation: A wh- question is reported with statement word order: the auxiliary 'does' disappears and the verb backshifts ('leave' becomes 'left'). There is no question mark and no comma before the reported clause.

items.prompt: "You should see a doctor about that cough," my friend told me. (reported speech)
answer: My friend told me (that) I should see a doctor about that cough.
My friend told me that I should see a doctor about that cough.
My friend told me I should see a doctor about that cough.
items.explanation: The modal 'should' has no past form and stays unchanged in reported speech; only the pronoun shifts ('you' becomes 'I'). Note that 'tell' takes a personal object ('told me'), unlike 'say'.

items.prompt: "We are moving house next month," they told us. (reported speech)
answer: They told us (that) they were moving house the following month.
They told us that they were moving house the following month.
They told us they were moving house the following month.
They told us (that) they were moving house the next month.
They told us they were moving house the next month.
items.explanation: Present continuous 'are moving' backshifts to past continuous 'were moving', 'we' becomes 'they', and 'next month' becomes 'the following month' / 'the next month'.

items.prompt: "Wait for me here," she said to him. (reported command)
answer: She told him to wait for her there.
She asked him to wait for her there.
items.explanation: Reported commands and requests use 'tell/ask someone to do' something; 'me' becomes 'her' and the place word 'here' shifts to 'there'.
